Severe decline in terrestrial insect species richness, abundance, flying biomass, and local
extinctions across Europe are cause for alarm. Here, we summarize this decline, and identify species affected most.

Antwortvariable.evidence_anchor: The clusters contained all possible combinations of five broadleaved tree species in one-, two- and three-species clusters (three diversity levels), replicated fourfold (20 one-species, 40 two-species and 40 three-species clusters).
Antwortvariable.first_evidence_snippet:
and species
composition in the Hainich forest in central Germany. The clusters contained all possible combinations of five
broadleaved tree species in one-, two- and three-species clusters (three diversity levels), replicated fourfold (20
one-species, 40 two-species and 40 three-species clusters). We estimated soil water uptake during a summer
dry period

Hauptgruppe_RoteListen.evidence_anchor: Observed tree species on these clusters were Acer pseudoplatanus (sycamore maple), Carpinus betulus (hornbeam), Fagus sylvatica (European beech), Fraxinus excelsior (ash) and Tilia sp. (lime).

Lebensraum.evidence_anchor: The study was conducted in the deciduous Hainich forest in central Germany close to the village of Weberstedt.

Trend.evidence_anchor: Besides a slightly higher water use of Fraxinus clusters, we could not detect any effects of species identity or diversity on cluster water use.

Untergruppe_RoteListen.evidence_anchor: Observed tree species on these clusters were Acer pseudoplatanus (sycamore maple), Carpinus betulus (hornbeam), Fagus sylvatica (European beech), Fraxinus excelsior (ash) and Tilia sp. (lime).

Trend.evidence_anchor: The morphotype and phylotype Shannon diversity indices were quite similar (Figure 4), but the phylotype diversity indices showed a smaller decline following repeated droughts. In the control plots, Shannon diversity indices were generally higher in the topsoil than in the deep layers (morphotype: 1.24 ± 0.04 vs. 0.92 ± 0.06, respectively, p < .05 in all years; phylotype: 1.39 ± 0.04 vs. 1.23 ± 0.05, respectively, p < .05 in 2014; Table 1). The effect of throughfall exclusion on ECM fungal diversity indices differed between beech and spruce, irrespective of the competitive situation. In the throughfall exclusion plots, the ECM fungal diversity indices remained unchanged in the first 2 years of drought for beech but declined from the second year of drought (2015) onwards for spruce. In 2016, there was a significant difference in the morphotype diversity indices between beech (control: 1.01 ± 0.08; throughfall exclusion: 0.80 ± 0.02) and spruce (control: 0.93 ± 0.21; throughfall exclusion: 0.27 ± 0.11) depending on the treatment (throughfall exclusion × BB vs. SS interaction: p < .01), with a much stronger decline being observed in spruce.

Hauptgruppe_RoteListen.first_evidence_snippet:
Stefan Brunzel, Alexandra Aparicio, Mathias Lohr und Burkhard Beinlich


**Abstracts**



In den Mittelgebirgsregionen Märkisches Sauerland und Westliches Weserbergland in Nordrhein-Westfalen wurde entlang
von Transekten ein Tagfalter-Monitoring durchgeführt. Die
daraus resultierenden Abundanzdaten wurden Trendanalysen
unterzogen. Zudem wurde der

Lebensraum.evidence_anchor: Arten, die geschlossene Wälder und Gebüsche mittlerer Standorte bevorzugen, nehmen im Verhältnis zu Arten der Magerrasen zu.
Lebensraum.first_evidence_snippet:
der Häufigkeiten in Abhängigkeit der Lebensraumpräferenzen der Arten stark unterscheiden. Arten, die geschlossene Wälder und Gebüsche mittlerer Standorte bevorzugen,
nehmen im Verhältnis zu Arten der Magerrasen zu.

Antwortvariable.evidence_anchor: Im Verlauf der fünfundzwanzigjährigen Untersuchung konnten wir in den von uns regelmäßig kontrollierten Winterquartieren zehn Fledermausarten mit 1436 Individuen nachweisen (vgl. Tabelle 1).
Antwortvariable.first_evidence_snippet:
der Gesamtzahl aller Untersuchungsjahre (n = 25)

## 4 Ergebnisse

Im Verlauf der fünfundzwanzigjährigen Untersuchung konnten wir in den von

Trend.evidence_anchor: In parallel to this decline of open pine forests also other habitat types were affected, in particular xerotherm fringe vegetation along the edges of the pine forests and on dolomite reefs inside the forests with their rock vegetation. Here, very rare and endangered (red list) species occur, e.g. Orobanche coerulescens (the only occurrence in Germany), the grasshopper species Psophus stridulus as well as relicts of the ice age (Cardaminopsis hispida and Podisma pedestris, another grasshopper). All these species had massive losses in their population numbers and sizes during the last decades.

Antwortvariable.evidence_anchor: Our data on raccoon distribution cover this period of conspicuous increase and allow us to study changes in density and distribution from 2000/01 to 2011/12 (Fig. 2, Online Resource Fig. S1).

Hauptgruppe_RoteListen.evidence_anchor: As the second largest cause of biodiversity loss worldwide, there is an urgent need to study the dynamics of biological invasions and identify factors limiting the distribution of invasive alien species. In the present study we analyze national-scale hunting bag data from Germany to predict the dispersal of raccoons in the largest non-native population of the species.

Lebensraum.evidence_anchor: The forested Solling mountains probably provide the most comparable habitat to that typically occupied in the native range, and population densities here are 1 – 4 individuals per 100 ha (Hohmann 1998).

Trend.evidence_anchor: The increase from about 9,000 harvested raccoons in 2000/01 to about 71,000 in 2011/12 reflects the extensive amount of suitable habitat for this omnivorous species in Central Europe.

Antwortvariable.evidence_anchor: The results indicate that open landscape species are particularly threatened. With exception of Lumbricidae (only two threatened species in Germany), species of open landscapes are overrepresented in the group of threatened species in all species groups (Fig. 2). Across all 8 higher taxa, the largest fraction of threatened species is found among species only occurring in open landscapes (O; about 50%). The lowest fraction of threatened species with known forest affinity is found in species predominantly living in forests (F; about 21%). Species occurring in forests and open landscapes but not predominantly in forests (M) show an intermediate percentage of threatened species (about 25%).

Antwortvariable.evidence_anchor: The forest affinity patterns change significantly from forest interior to the outermost margin of the forest edge in a predictable manner. Species predominantly occurring in forests (categories: f, fc, fl) are dominant in the forest interior (61% of species and 79% of specimens) and strongly decrease towards the “outer area dominated by herbaceous plants” (18% of the species and 6% of the specimens) (Fig. 3). Similarly, species exclusively living in open habitats (o) are nearly absent in the forest interior (0.7% of species and 0.1% of specimens) but strongly increase towards the “outer area dominated by herbs” (19% of the species and 14% of specimens) (Fig. 3).

Temporal dynamics from 1960 to 2012


The herb layer species that increase from 1960 to 2012 are
mostly woody species and herbaceous true forest species,

Antwortvariable.evidence_anchor: Alpha-diversity on study sites was described using three metrics (data of individual traps per study site were pooled): abundance (number of individuals), species density (number of species, sensu Gotelli & Colwell, 2001), and species richness (number of species, accounting for abundance, sensu Gotelli & Colwell, 2001) (package “vegan” by Oksanen et al., 2020).

Hauptgruppe_RoteListen.evidence_anchor: Dung beetles are important actors in the self-regulation of ecosystems by driving nutrient cycling, bioturbation, and pest suppression.

Lebensraum.evidence_anchor: Each of the 44 study regions (à 5.8 km × 5.8 km) was assigned to the dominant regional landscape type (near-natural, agricultural, urban). The regional landscape types consisted of 16 near-natural landscapes (defined as >85% near-natural vegetation including a minimum of 50% forest)

Trend.evidence_anchor: Our results show that abundance and species density of dung-visiting beetles were negatively affected by agricultural land use at both spatial scales, whereas γ-diversity at the local scale was negatively affected by settlements and on a landscape scale equally by agricultural and urban land use.

Untergruppe_RoteListen.evidence_anchor: Dung beetles are important actors in the self-regulation of ecosystems by driving nutrient cycling, bioturbation, and pest suppression.

Antwortvariable.evidence_anchor: We performed separated sample-based rarefaction-extrapolations (package “iNEXT,” Hsieh et al., 2020) along the Hill numbers (q = 0, 1, and 2) (Hill, 1973). Because Hill numbers imply mathematical properties that allow drawing conclusions about diversity across different diversity indices (Chao, Chiu, et al., 2014; Jost, 2006), there seems to be broad agreement on the use of Hill numbers to quantify species diversity (Ellison, 2010).

Hauptgruppe_RoteListen.evidence_anchor: Dung beetles are important actors in the self-regulation of ecosystems by driving nutrient cycling, bioturbation, and pest suppression.

Lebensraum.evidence_anchor: Each of the 44 study regions (à 5.8 km × 5.8 km) was assigned to the dominant regional landscape type (near-natural, agricultural, urban). The regional landscape types consisted of 16 near-natural landscapes (defined as >85% near-natural vegetation including a minimum of 50% forest)

Trend.evidence_anchor: The rarefaction interpolation curves for q = 0 on the habitat scale showed no distinctive pattern for rare species diversity. At a landscape level, rare species diversity tended to be lower in agricultural and urban landscapes than in near-natural landscapes, but this difference was not significant (Figure 4).

Untergruppe_RoteListen.evidence_anchor: Dung beetles are important actors in the self-regulation of ecosystems by driving nutrient cycling, bioturbation, and pest suppression.
